articles.title: Founders Talk EP3: The Truth About Entrepreneurship Nobody Tells You
articles.subtitle: The success stories are real. But so are the parts nobody talks about — the isolation, the self-doubt, the months where nothing works, and the version of yourself you have to become before the business becomes what you dreamed.
articles.author: Sasha Gaye Hemmings | LWS Media Network
articles.coverImageCaption: Founders Talk Episode 3 — Sasha Gaye Hemmings. Photo: LWS Media Network
articles.excerpt: The success stories are real. But so are the parts nobody talks about — the isolation, the self-doubt, the months where nothing works, and the version of yourself you have to become before the business becomes what you dreamed.

body.text: Welcome back to Founders Talk. I am Sasha Gaye Hemmings and today we are having a different kind of conversation. Over the past two weeks we talked about why people never start and what I wish I had known before launching my first business. Today I want to go somewhere even more honest. I want to talk about the things nobody tells you about entrepreneurship — the parts that get edited out of the success stories, the parts that almost broke me, and the parts that ultimately made me.

I want to say upfront — this is not a discouragement from building. If anything, I hope it is the opposite. Because the founders who make it are not the ones who never struggled. They are the ones who struggled and kept going anyway. But they deserve to know what they are getting into. You deserve to know what you are getting into.
Nobody Talks About the Loneliness

Entrepreneurship is one of the loneliest things I have ever done. Not because I did not have people around me — I did. But because the specific experience of building a business, carrying its weight, making decisions that affect your livelihood and potentially others, sitting with uncertainty at 2am — very few people in your life will truly understand that unless they have lived it themselves.

The people who love you will try. They will listen. They will encourage you. But there is a particular kind of isolation that comes with being the one who has to figure it out — who cannot fully off-load the burden because the business depends on you and only you. I learned to be more intentional about surrounding myself with other founders. People who do not need the experience explained. People who just get it.
Find your people. Not just people who support you — people who understand you. There is a difference. And in entrepreneurship, that difference is everything.
The Self-Doubt Never Fully Goes Away

I want to tell you that after a certain point — after enough clients, enough wins, enough evidence that you can do this — the self-doubt disappears. But that would not be true. What changes is your relationship with it. You learn to act despite it rather than waiting for it to leave. You learn to recognise it as fear, not as fact. You learn that the voice that says you are not enough is not a reliable narrator.

I have multiple degrees. I have helped over 134 clients build successful businesses. I have published books. I have built multiple companies simultaneously as a solo founder. And I still have moments of doubt. Still have days where I question whether I am doing enough, building fast enough, thinking big enough. The difference now is I do not let those moments make my decisions. I feel them, acknowledge them, and keep moving.
There Will Be Months Where Nothing Works

This is perhaps the most important thing I can tell any aspiring entrepreneur. There will be months — maybe several in a row — where despite your best efforts, your hardest work, and your most strategic thinking, the results simply do not come. Clients do not convert. Revenue stalls. Something you were counting on falls through. The plan you were executing stops working.

These months are not evidence that you are failing. They are part of the process. Every business I have ever studied — and I have studied many — has these seasons. What separates the businesses that survive them from the ones that do not is rarely strategy or talent. It is usually resilience. The ability to stay in motion even when the motion does not yet feel like progress.

I grew up learning resilience the hard way long before I started a business. The conditions of my childhood in Jamaica demanded it. In a strange way that background — the hardship, the years of navigating scarcity and uncertainty — prepared me for entrepreneurship better than any business course could have. When you have already survived hard things, the hard seasons in business become more manageable. Not easy. But survivable.
People Will Not Always Believe in You — And That Is Not the Problem

Some of the people closest to you will not understand what you are building. Some will question it openly. Some will offer advice disguised as concern that is really just their own fear being projected onto your dream. Some will wait quietly to see if you fail before deciding whether to support you.

This used to hurt me more than it does now. What I have come to understand is that other people's inability to see your vision is not a reflection of the vision — it is a reflection of their own limitations. Not everyone has the capacity to imagine something that does not yet exist. That is not a character flaw. It is just a human limitation. Your job is not to convince them. Your job is to build the thing and let the results speak.
The Business Will Demand You Become Someone New

This is perhaps the most profound truth about entrepreneurship that nobody prepares you for. The business you are trying to build requires a version of you that does not yet fully exist. You will need to develop confidence you do not currently have. You will need to learn skills that are currently outside your comfort zone. You will need to make decisions that terrify you and then live with the consequences either way.

Entrepreneurship is the most accelerated personal development programme on the planet. It will expose every limitation, every fear, every blind spot you have — and it will demand that you address them if you want to grow. The business does not just ask for your time and your talent. It asks for your full self. The version of you that shows up on day one of your business is rarely the version of you that is capable of running the business you are trying to build. The journey between those two versions is the real work.
You are not just building a business. You are building yourself. And sometimes — honestly, often — that is the harder and more important project of the two.
And Yet — It Is Worth It

After everything I have described — the loneliness, the doubt, the slow seasons, the people who did not believe, the constant demand to grow into someone bigger than you currently are — I want to be absolutely clear: I would do it all again. Without hesitation.

Because on the other side of all of that is something that employment can never give you. It is the ownership of your own time, your own decisions, your own direction. It is the ability to build something that reflects your values and serves the people you actually want to serve. It is watching an idea that lived only in your mind become something real that impacts real people. That feeling — that particular combination of terror and pride and purpose — is unlike anything else I have ever experienced.

I built Creative Minds Movement Services from nothing and helped over 134 clients do the same. I built Learning With Samara as a solo founder from the ground up. I built LWS Media Network — this platform — from an idea to a fully functioning global media company. I did all of this while raising children, writing books, and teaching students. Not because it was easy. Because it was mine.

That is the truth about entrepreneurship that nobody tells you loudly enough. Yes it is hard. Yes it will test you in ways you cannot currently anticipate. And yes — for the right person, with the right commitment — it is the most powerful and fulfilling thing you will ever do.

Thank you for being here for Founders Talk. Every week I will be in this space giving you the real conversation. No filters. No highlight reel. Just truth, insight, and the genuine desire to see you build something remarkable. See you next week.

articles.title: Founders Talk EP2: What I Wish I Knew Before Starting My First Business in 2018
articles.subtitle: If I could sit down with myself on the day I registered Creative Minds Movement Services and give her one hour of honest advice — this is exactly what I would say.
articles.author: Sasha Gaye Hemmings | LWS Media Network
articles.coverImageCaption: Founders Talk Episode 2 — Sasha Gaye Hemmings. Photo: LWS Media Network
articles.excerpt: If I could sit down with myself on the day I registered Creative Minds Movement Services and give her one hour of honest advice — this is exactly what I would say. No sugar coating. No highlight reel. Just the truth.

body.text: Welcome back to Founders Talk. I am Sasha Gaye Hemmings and this is the weekly space where I give you real, honest, no-fluff business insight from someone who has been building in the trenches since 2018. Last week we talked about why most people never start. This week I want to go deeper — and more personal. I want to talk about what I actually wish I had known before I launched my first business.

When I started Creative Minds Movement Services in 2018 I had degrees. I had knowledge. I had passion and drive and a genuine belief that I could build something meaningful. What I did not have was the full picture of what entrepreneurship actually looks like from the inside. There are things business school teaches you and there are things life teaches you. The second category is far more expensive and far more valuable.
1. Your Network Is Your Net Worth — Build It Before You Need It

I learned this the hard way. When you need clients, when you need collaborators, when you need someone to vouch for you — that is not the time to start building relationships. That time is before. Relationships built out of desperation feel like desperation. Relationships built over time, with genuine interest and reciprocity, become the foundation of a sustainable business.

I wish I had invested more time early on in showing up for other people — attending events, connecting with peers in my field, offering value before I needed anything in return. The clients I have retained longest, the partnerships that have meant the most — almost all of them came through relationships that were built quietly, without any immediate agenda.
2. Pricing Yourself Correctly Is an Act of Self-Respect

This one is painful to admit but I am going to say it anyway because I know I am not alone in this. When I started, I underpriced my services. Not because I did not know my value — but because I was afraid. Afraid clients would say no. Afraid I was not established enough to charge what my expertise was actually worth. That fear cost me time, energy, and money.

Here is what I know now: the right clients do not run from your price. The wrong clients always will. When you underprice yourself you attract people who do not value what you offer. When you price correctly you attract people who are serious, committed, and respectful of your work. Your price is a filter. Use it intentionally.
Undercharging is not humility. It is self-sabotage dressed up as accessibility. Know your worth. Charge accordingly. Apologise to no one.
3. Systems Save Businesses. Passion Starts Them.

Passion will get you out of bed at 5am to work on your business. Passion will push you through the hard days. But passion alone will not keep a business running. What keeps a business running is systems — repeatable processes for onboarding clients, delivering services, following up, invoicing, managing time, and tracking results.

I spent too long running everything from my head. Every process lived in my memory rather than in a documented system. That works when you are small and everything is manageable. It breaks down the moment volume increases or you need someone else to step in. Build your systems early. Document your processes. Treat your business like it is going to grow — because if you do the work right, it will.
4. Not Every Client Is the Right Client

This took me longer to learn than I would like to admit. In the early days I said yes to almost everyone because I needed the revenue and I genuinely wanted to help. But not every client respects your boundaries, values your expertise, or operates in a way that is aligned with how you work. Some clients cost you more — in time, energy, and peace of mind — than they pay you.

Learning to qualify clients before committing — to ask the right questions, to notice the early warning signs, and to say no when a fit is not right — was one of the most transformative things I did for my business. The client list I am most proud of is not the longest one I have ever had. It is the one built on alignment, mutual respect, and genuine results.
5. Rest Is Part of the Strategy

Entrepreneurship culture celebrates hustle. It glorifies the grind. It treats rest like a reward you have not yet earned. I bought into that narrative for longer than I should have and it cost me. Burnout is real. It does not announce itself politely — it arrives quietly and then all at once, taking your creativity, your clarity, and your energy with it.

I am a mother, an educator, a founder, a consultant, an author, and a media publisher simultaneously. I have to be intentional about rest in a way that the hustle culture content I consumed early in my journey never taught me. Rest is not the absence of productivity. It is the prerequisite for it. Your best ideas, your clearest decisions, and your most powerful work come from a rested mind.
6. Your Story Is an Asset — Use It

I grew up with very little. My journey to where I am today was not smooth or linear or handed to me. For a long time I was hesitant to talk about that publicly. I worried it would make me seem less credible, less polished, less like someone people should trust with their business.

I was wrong. Your story is not a liability. It is your most powerful differentiator. There is no marketing campaign more compelling than a true account of someone who came from difficult circumstances and built something real. People do not just buy your service — they buy your story, your conviction, and their belief that if you could do it, maybe they can too.
I wish I had known sooner that the very things I tried to hide about my journey were the same things that would one day make people trust me most.
The Bottom Line

If I could go back to 2018 and sit with the version of myself about to start her first business, I would tell her this: the road is harder than you think and more rewarding than you can currently imagine. Price yourself right from the beginning. Build real relationships before you need them. Document your processes. Say no to the wrong clients so you have capacity for the right ones. Rest without guilt. And for the love of everything — tell your story. All of it. Because it is the most powerful thing you have.

Next week on Founders Talk we are getting into the truth about entrepreneurship that nobody tells you — the parts that the success stories leave out and the parts that almost broke me before they made me stronger. You do not want to miss it. See you then.

articles.title: Founders Talk EP1: Why Most People Never Start — And What To Do About It
articles.subtitle: Every week I sit down and talk business — real, honest, no-fluff conversations about entrepreneurship, building, and the mindset it takes to create something from nothing. This is Episode 1.
articles.author: Sasha Gaye Hemmings | LWS Media Network
articles.coverImageCaption: Founders Talk — Weekly business insights from Sasha Gaye Hemmings. Photo: LWS Media Network
articles.excerpt: Most people who want to start a business never do. Not because they lack talent. Not because they lack ideas. But because of something far more invisible and far more powerful — fear dressed up as practicality.

body.text: Welcome to Founders Talk — a weekly segment here on LWS Media Network where I sit down and talk business. Real conversations. No fluff. No curated highlight reel. Just honest, practical insight from someone who has been in the trenches of entrepreneurship since 2018, built businesses from the ground up, helped over 134 clients do the same, and learned — sometimes the hard way — what it actually takes to build something that lasts.

I am Sasha Gaye Hemmings. Founder. Educator. Consultant. Author. And every week in this space I am going to give you something useful. Something you can take away and apply. This is Episode 1 — and we are starting with the question I get asked most often, in one form or another, by almost everyone who comes to me wanting to build something of their own.
The Question Nobody Wants to Ask Out Loud

Why do most people never start? It is the elephant in the room of every entrepreneurship conversation. We talk about business plans, funding, marketing strategies, branding — all of it useful, all of it necessary. But none of it matters if you never actually begin. And the uncomfortable truth is that most people who say they want to start a business never do.

I have sat across from hundreds of aspiring entrepreneurs over the years. Talented people. Intelligent people. People with genuinely good ideas and real potential. And the single biggest thing that separates the ones who build something from the ones who do not is not money. It is not connections. It is not the right moment or the right circumstances. It is the decision to start despite not feeling ready.

Nobody feels ready. Readiness is not a feeling — it is a decision. The people who build things are not the ones who waited until they felt confident. They are the ones who moved while they were still afraid.
Fear Dressed Up as Practicality

Here is what I have learned: most people do not say they are afraid. They say they are being practical. They say they are waiting for the right time. They say they need more money, more experience, more research, more certainty. And all of those things sound reasonable. They are not unreasonable thoughts. But in most cases — not all, but most — they are fear dressed up in practical clothing.

Fear of failure. Fear of judgement. Fear of investing everything and losing it. Fear of what people will say if it does not work out. Fear of discovering that the idea they have been holding onto — the one that feels like it could be something — might not be as good as they hoped. These are real fears. I have felt all of them personally. I still feel some of them. The difference is I stopped letting them make my decisions for me.
The Myth of the Perfect Moment

One of the most damaging ideas in entrepreneurship is the idea that there is a perfect moment to start. There is not. There has never been. There will never be. Life does not pause to give you a clear runway. The bills do not stop while you get ready. The responsibilities do not disappear. The uncertainty does not resolve itself before you take the first step.

I started Creative Minds Movement Services in 2018. Was it the perfect moment? No. I faced real obstacles. I did not have everything figured out. What I had was a decision — a commitment to myself that I was going to do this. That I was going to show up even when it was uncomfortable, even when I was uncertain, even when the path was not clear. The clarity came from moving. Not from waiting.
What You Actually Need to Start

You need an idea that solves a real problem for real people. You need enough knowledge to begin — not to finish, just to begin. You need a willingness to learn what you do not yet know. And you need the courage to be imperfect in public while you figure it out. That is it. Everything else — the logo, the website, the business plan, the office — those come later. They are not prerequisites. They are the things you build as you go.

The entrepreneurs I have watched build the most remarkable things did not wait until they had all the answers. They started with a question — how can I solve this problem? — and they kept asking it every single day until a business formed around the answer. That is the process. It is messy and nonlinear and nothing like what they teach in business school. But it is real.
A Word to Anyone Sitting on an Idea Right Now

If you are reading this and you have an idea you have been sitting on — weeks, months, maybe years — I want to speak directly to you. The fact that the idea is still there means something. Ideas that are not meant for you do not stick around. They fade. But the ones that keep coming back, the ones that wake you up at night, the ones you think about in quiet moments — those are worth taking seriously.

You do not have to quit your job tomorrow. You do not have to have the funding or the perfect plan. But you do have to do something. Write it down. Tell one trusted person. Register the business name. Buy the domain. Do one small concrete thing that makes it real. Because the moment it becomes real — even slightly, even imperfectly — it stops being a dream and starts being a business.
The world does not need more people with good ideas. It needs more people willing to do something with them. Start imperfectly. Start scared. Just start.
Next Week on Founders Talk

Next week we are talking about what I wish I had known before starting my first business in 2018. The lessons that cost me time, energy, and in some cases money — so you do not have to learn them the hard way. Join me every week right here on LWS Media Network for Founders Talk — because building something of your own is one of the most powerful things a human being can do. And you deserve real guidance along the way.

articles.title: I Refused to Stay Small — The Story of Sasha Gaye Hemmings
articles.subtitle: From a difficult childhood in Jamaica to building multiple businesses, publishing books, and raising a daughter who is already changing the world — this is my story, told in my own words.
articles.author: Sasha Gaye Hemmings | LWS Media Network
articles.coverImageCaption: Sasha Gaye Hemmings — Founder, Educator, Author, and Editor in Chief of LWS Media Network. Photo: LWS Media Network
articles.excerpt: From a difficult childhood in Jamaica to building multiple businesses, publishing books, and raising a daughter who is already changing the world — this is my story, in my own words. I refused to stay small. And I am just getting started.

body.text: I want to start by saying something that took me a long time to believe about myself: I am enough. Not in spite of where I came from. Not despite the hardships, the setbacks, the nights I was not sure how things would work out. I am enough because of all of it. Every single piece of my story — the painful parts, the proud parts, and everything in between — made me exactly who I am today. And who I am today is someone who refuses to be defined by limitations.
Where It Started

I grew up in Jamaica. And I want to be honest about what that means for me personally — it was not easy. My childhood was marked by harsh conditions, real struggles, and circumstances that could have broken me if I had allowed them to. Many people in situations like mine do not make it out. They get swallowed by the weight of what surrounds them. I understand that. I lived inside that weight.

But somewhere inside me there was always a voice that said: this is not the whole story. There is more. You are more. I held onto that voice even when everything around me said otherwise. Looking back now, I think that voice was the truest thing about me. It was the part of me that knew — before I had any evidence to prove it — that I was built for something bigger.

I believe we are multidimensional beings. We are not meant to be one thing. We are not meant to be boxed in. The full human experience is available to all of us — and I made a decision early that I was going to claim as much of it as I possibly could.
Education Was My First Act of Defiance

I pursued education with a ferocity that surprised even people who knew me. Not because someone told me to. Not because it was expected. But because I understood instinctively that knowledge was the one thing no circumstance could take from me. I earned my Bachelor of Science in Business Administration with a major in Human Resources. Then I went further — completing a Masters degree in Education and another in Business. I also earned my TESOL certification, qualifying me to teach English as a second language to students around the world.

People sometimes look at that list and think it is impressive. What they do not always see is the sacrifice behind it. The late nights. The moments of doubt. The times I wondered if it was worth it. It was always worth it. Because every qualification I earned was not just a credential — it was proof to myself that I could do hard things. And once you prove that to yourself once, you never forget it.
Building Creative Minds Movement Services

In 2018 I founded Creative Minds Movement Services — my first business and the foundation upon which everything else has been built. Through Creative Minds I offer expert-level services in business consulting, human resources, management, organisational development, and English language instruction. It is a business born directly from my education, my experience, and my genuine passion for helping others build something of their own.

What I am most proud of is not the business itself — it is the results. Over the years Creative Minds Movement Services has helped more than 134 clients build and grow their businesses successfully. A high retention rate that speaks not just to the quality of the work, but to the relationships built along the way. When someone trusts you with their business — their livelihood, their dream — that is not something I take lightly. I have never taken it lightly.

Teaching has always been at the heart of what I do. I teach English to foreign students. I teach business fundamentals to aspiring entrepreneurs. I teach children and adults across multiple subjects. Education is not just a service I offer — it is who I am. It runs through everything. It is why my daughter Samara grew up understanding that learning is not a burden. It is a superpower.

134 clients built successfully. A high retention rate. But the number I am most proud of is not a client count — it is the number of people who found their confidence, their direction, and their voice through the work we did together.
The Author

I am also an author. Writing has always been one of the ways I make sense of the world — and one of the ways I give back to it. I have published five books spanning business, science, education, and practical educational worksheets designed to make learning more accessible and engaging. One of them, Sasha's Module for Success, is available on Amazon. It represents my philosophy in a form that anyone can hold in their hands and apply to their own life.

Writing does not happen in a vacuum. It requires you to sit with yourself long enough to figure out what you actually believe. Every book I have written has taught me something about my own thinking. And every book I have yet to publish — and there are more — represents a part of me still working itself out onto the page.

My daughter Samara has followed in those footsteps. At nine years old she is already a published author. Her book Prince Leo and the Dragon's Promise Flicker is available on Amazon. That is not something I pushed her into. That is something that grew naturally from a home where reading, learning, and creating were always treated as the most valuable things you could do with your time.
Learning With Samara and LWS Media Network

Learning With Samara is an AI-powered educational platform for children ages 0 to 17. It covers curriculum from PEP Jamaica prep to ESL for non-English speakers, gamified learning, and a growing library of tools designed to make education feel like an adventure rather than a chore. I built it as a solo founder. Every line of code, every decision, every feature — handled by me, informed by my years of experience as an educator and a businesswoman.

And now there is LWS Media Network — this platform. A global multimedia network built to tell the stories that matter. The stories of innovators and founders. The stories that mainstream media ignores. The science our planet needs us to understand. The news delivered with honesty and perspective. And yes — stories like this one. Stories of people who started with very little and built something real.

I did not build all of this because it was easy. I built it because I could not imagine not building it. Because every experience I have ever had — every lesson learned in a classroom, every client I have ever sat across from, every hard night I survived in Jamaica — pointed me in this direction. This is not luck. This is a life's work.
My Children Are My Why

People often ask what drives me. The answer has never changed. My children are the driving force behind everything I do. When I build a platform, I am building something they can look at and know — our family creates. When I publish a book, I am showing them that your voice deserves to be heard. When I start a business, I am demonstrating that you do not wait for permission to build the life you want.

Samara already understands this at nine years old. She has a YouTube channel, a published book, and a learning platform named after her. Not because I handed her those things — but because she grew up in an environment where building was treated as natural. Where ideas were taken seriously. Where the question was never whether you could do something, but how.
What I Know For Sure

I know that the conditions you are born into do not determine where you end. I know that education is the most powerful tool available to anyone willing to use it. I know that being multidimensional is not a flaw — it is a gift. The world will try to put you in a box. It will tell you to pick one thing, stay in your lane, be realistic. I have never listened to that voice. I never intend to.

I am an entrepreneur. An educator. An author. A media founder. A mother. A consultant. A Jamaican woman who grew up with very little and built more than most people dream of. And I am not finished. Not even close.
You are allowed to be everything this human experience makes possible. Do not let anyone — or anything — convince you otherwise.

articles.title: El Nino and the Planet on Fire — What the World Needs to Understand About Our Changing Climate
articles.subtitle: El Nino is not just a weather pattern. It is a warning. As its effects intensify across the Caribbean, the Americas, the UK, and beyond, the world can no longer afford to treat climate change as a future problem.
articles.author: LWS Media Network
articles.coverImageCaption: El Nino is reshaping weather patterns across the entire planet. Photo: LWS Media Network
articles.excerpt: El Nino is not just a weather pattern. It is a warning. As its effects intensify across the Caribbean, the Americas, the UK, and beyond, the world can no longer afford to treat climate change as a future problem.

body.text: Every few years, the Pacific Ocean warms. Trade winds weaken. Weather patterns shift. Scientists call it El Nino — a Spanish phrase meaning The Little Boy, or The Christ Child, named by South American fishermen who first noticed unusually warm coastal waters around Christmas. But there is nothing little about what El Nino does to the planet. And in an era of accelerating climate change, its effects are becoming more severe, more unpredictable, and more dangerous than at any point in recorded history.
What Exactly Is El Nino?

El Nino is part of a natural climate cycle called the El Nino-Southern Oscillation, or ENSO. Under normal conditions, strong trade winds blow warm surface water westward across the Pacific Ocean, allowing cooler water to rise along the South American coast. During El Nino, those trade winds weaken or even reverse. Warm water spreads eastward across the central and eastern Pacific, raising ocean temperatures by as much as 3 to 5 degrees Celsius above average in some regions.

This shift in ocean temperatures does not stay contained to the Pacific. It disrupts atmospheric circulation patterns around the entire planet, triggering a cascade of weather extremes — droughts in some regions, catastrophic flooding in others, intensified hurricanes, unusual cold snaps, and prolonged heatwaves that push ecosystems and human communities to their limits.

El Nino does not cause climate change. But climate change makes El Nino more dangerous. A warmer baseline means every El Nino event now starts from a higher temperature floor — and the consequences are amplified accordingly.
The Caribbean — Between Drought and Destruction

For the Caribbean, El Nino brings a cruel paradox. On one hand, it tends to suppress Atlantic hurricane activity during its peak phase by increasing wind shear — the difference in wind speed and direction at different altitudes that tears developing storms apart before they can strengthen. For hurricane-weary islands, this might sound like good news. But the reality is far more complicated.

El Nino brings severe drought conditions to much of the Caribbean. Jamaica, Barbados, Trinidad and Tobago, and other islands experience significantly reduced rainfall during El Nino events, leading to water shortages, failed crops, and serious threats to food security. Farmers across the region have reported devastating losses as rainfall deficits stretch for months.

Coral reefs — among the Caribbean's most vital ecosystems and a cornerstone of its tourism economy — suffer dramatically during El Nino. Warmer ocean temperatures cause mass coral bleaching events, where stressed corals expel the algae living in their tissues and turn white. Prolonged bleaching kills coral. The Caribbean has lost more than half of its coral reef cover over the past four decades, and El Nino events accelerate that destruction.

The transition period after El Nino ends is also dangerous. As the climate system swings back toward neutral or La Nina conditions — El Nino's cooler counterpart — Atlantic hurricane seasons can intensify dramatically. The Caribbean goes from drought to the threat of catastrophic storms in a matter of months, leaving communities with little time to recover between crises.
The Americas — Floods, Fires, and Famine

Across North and South America, El Nino writes very different stories depending on where you are. The western United States and the Gulf Coast typically see heavier than normal rainfall during El Nino events, which can bring much-needed relief to drought-stricken regions like California — but also devastating floods, mudslides, and infrastructure damage to communities unprepared for the deluge.

The Pacific Northwest and Canada, by contrast, tend to experience warmer and drier conditions during El Nino. This amplifies wildfire risk in forests already stressed by years of drought and rising temperatures. The catastrophic wildfire seasons that have become a defining feature of North American summers in recent years are closely linked to El Nino's drying influence combined with the background warming driven by climate change.

In South America, the impacts are severe and wide-ranging. Peru and Ecuador face extreme flooding and landslides as unusually warm Pacific waters supercharge rainfall along the coast. Brazil's northeastern region, already one of the most drought-prone areas on earth, suffers prolonged dry spells that devastate agriculture and push communities into food insecurity. The Amazon — the planet's largest rainforest and a critical carbon sink — faces increased fire risk as dry conditions spread.

Central America faces some of the most acute food security challenges. The Dry Corridor — a stretch running through Guatemala, Honduras, El Salvador, and Nicaragua — is particularly vulnerable to El Nino-related drought. Smallholder farmers who depend on seasonal rains lose entire harvests. Malnutrition rates rise. Migration pressures increase as communities can no longer sustain themselves on the land.
The United Kingdom — The Ripple Effect Reaches Europe

The United Kingdom sits far from the Pacific Ocean, but El Nino's reach extends even to northwest Europe through complex atmospheric connections. Research has shown that strong El Nino events are associated with wetter and milder winters in the UK and northwestern Europe, as the jet stream — the high-altitude band of fast-moving air that steers weather systems — shifts position in response to changes in tropical weather patterns.

For the UK this means increased flood risk during El Nino years. Rivers overflow. Coastal communities face storm surge threats. Infrastructure built for a climate that no longer exists struggles to cope. The UK has already experienced a series of record-breaking flood events in recent years, and El Nino amplifies the atmospheric conditions that produce them.

Summers following strong El Nino events can bring exceptional heat to Europe. The record-breaking heatwaves that have struck the continent in recent years — killing tens of thousands of people, buckling railway lines, and overwhelming healthcare systems — occur in a climate system where El Nino has contributed to pushing global temperatures to new highs.
The Planet — A System Under Stress

Zoom out to the planetary level and the picture becomes even more alarming. El Nino events add a temporary boost of warmth on top of the long-term warming trend driven by human greenhouse gas emissions. During strong El Nino years, global average temperatures can be pushed 0.2 to 0.3 degrees Celsius above the already elevated baseline. In a world that is already 1.2 degrees warmer than pre-industrial levels, this matters enormously.

The years 2023 and 2024 were the hottest ever recorded in human history — driven in part by a powerful El Nino event on top of decades of accumulated warming. Sea surface temperatures reached unprecedented levels. Antarctic sea ice fell to record lows. Glaciers retreated further and faster than any previous measurement had captured.

Global food systems face mounting pressure. El Nino disrupts agricultural production in multiple major food-producing regions simultaneously. When droughts hit the US Midwest, floods devastate South Asian rice paddies, and East African harvests fail all in the same year, the cascading effects on global food prices and food security are severe.
Climate Change Is Making It Worse

Here is the critical distinction that must be understood: El Nino is a natural phenomenon. It has been occurring for thousands of years. But climate change — driven by the burning of fossil fuels and the resulting buildup of carbon dioxide and other greenhouse gases in the atmosphere — is fundamentally altering how El Nino behaves and what it does to the planet.

A warmer ocean is a more energetic ocean. A warmer atmosphere holds more moisture. When El Nino triggers rainfall, it now triggers heavier rainfall. When El Nino triggers drought, it now triggers more severe drought. The natural cycle has not changed, but the world it operates in has. And that world is now running hotter than at any point in the past 125,000 years.
We are no longer just dealing with El Nino. We are dealing with El Nino in a world our grandparents would not recognize — and our grandchildren may not survive in without urgent action.
What Needs to Happen

The science is settled. The evidence is overwhelming. What is lacking is not knowledge but political will and collective urgency. Reducing greenhouse gas emissions remains the most critical long-term intervention — every fraction of a degree of warming prevented is lives saved, ecosystems preserved, and communities protected.

Small island developing states — including those across the Caribbean — must be centered in global climate finance discussions. These communities contribute minimally to global emissions but bear disproportionate consequences. The loss and damage funding mechanisms agreed at recent international climate summits must be fully funded and rapidly deployed.

Early warning systems for extreme weather events must be expanded globally, particularly in developing nations where communities have the least capacity to prepare for and recover from climate disasters. Infrastructure must be redesigned for the climate we now have, not the one we had fifty years ago.
El Nino is nature speaking. Climate change is the language we taught it. The question is whether we will listen before it is too late.

articles.title: Weh Yuh Seh Views: El Nino Is Here — And We Are Not Ready
articles.subtitle: While world leaders debate timelines and targets, El Nino is already reshaping life across the Caribbean, the Americas, and beyond. The time for talking is over.

body.text: Good morning. Welcome to Weh Yuh Seh Views. I am Sasha Hemmings, and today I want to talk about something that is affecting every single person on this planet right now — whether they know it or not. El Nino is here. And we are not ready.

Across the Caribbean, farmers are watching their crops fail in the drought. Coral reefs are bleaching and dying in waters that are too warm. In South America, communities are being swept away by floods. In North America, wildfires are burning hotter and longer. In the UK, flood records are being broken year after year. And across the Global South, food insecurity is rising to levels that should be triggering emergency responses from every government on earth.

El Nino is not a natural disaster we can shrug at. It is a natural phenomenon made catastrophic by human choices. And the people paying the highest price are the ones who made the fewest of those choices.
body.cite: Sasha Gaye Hemmings, LWS Media Network

body.text: What frustrates me — and what should frustrate all of us — is that none of this is new information. Scientists have been warning about the intensifying impact of El Nino in a warming world for decades. The data has been there. The projections have been clear. And yet here we are, watching the predictions come true in real time, while global climate negotiations continue to move at a pace that insults the urgency of the crisis.

For the Caribbean specifically, this is not an abstract conversation. We are talking about the islands where people live, where families grow food, where reefs sustain entire economies. Small island nations that have contributed almost nothing to global carbon emissions are now on the front lines of a crisis they did not create. That is not just an environmental injustice. It is a moral failure of the entire international system.

The coverage this crisis receives in mainstream media is nowhere near proportionate to its scale. We cover celebrity feuds for days. We dedicate entire news cycles to political drama that will be forgotten in a week. But the slow-motion destruction of the planet's climate system gets a feature every few months — and then we move on.

Not here. At LWS Media Network, climate is not a quarterly story. It is a beat. It is a responsibility. And we will keep reporting on it — from the Caribbean perspective, from the Global South perspective, from the perspective of the people most affected and least heard.
The planet is speaking. Are we listening? This is Weh Yuh Seh Views — stay alert, stay aware, and stay loud.

articles.title: El Nino and the Planet on Fire — What the World Needs to Understand About Our Changing Climate
articles.subtitle: El Nino is not just a weather pattern. It is a warning. As its effects intensify across the Caribbean, the Americas, the UK, and beyond, the world can no longer afford to treat climate change as a future problem.
articles.author: LWS Media Network
articles.coverImageCaption: El Nino is reshaping weather patterns across the entire planet. Photo: LWS Media Network
articles.excerpt: El Nino is not just a weather pattern. It is a warning. As its effects intensify across the Caribbean, the Americas, the UK, and beyond, the world can no longer afford to treat climate change as a future problem.

body.text: Every few years, the Pacific Ocean warms. Trade winds weaken. Weather patterns shift. Scientists call it El Nino — a Spanish phrase meaning The Little Boy, or The Christ Child, named by South American fishermen who first noticed unusually warm coastal waters around Christmas. But there is nothing little about what El Nino does to the planet. And in an era of accelerating climate change, its effects are becoming more severe, more unpredictable, and more dangerous than at any point in recorded history.
What Exactly Is El Nino?

El Nino is part of a natural climate cycle called the El Nino-Southern Oscillation, or ENSO. Under normal conditions, strong trade winds blow warm surface water westward across the Pacific Ocean, allowing cooler water to rise along the South American coast. During El Nino, those trade winds weaken or even reverse. Warm water spreads eastward across the central and eastern Pacific, raising ocean temperatures by as much as 3 to 5 degrees Celsius above average in some regions.

This shift in ocean temperatures does not stay contained to the Pacific. It disrupts atmospheric circulation patterns around the entire planet, triggering a cascade of weather extremes — droughts in some regions, catastrophic flooding in others, intensified hurricanes, unusual cold snaps, and prolonged heatwaves that push ecosystems and human communities to their limits.

El Nino does not cause climate change. But climate change makes El Nino more dangerous. A warmer baseline means every El Nino event now starts from a higher temperature floor — and the consequences are amplified accordingly.
The Caribbean — Between Drought and Destruction

For the Caribbean, El Nino brings a cruel paradox. On one hand, it tends to suppress Atlantic hurricane activity during its peak phase by increasing wind shear — the difference in wind speed and direction at different altitudes that tears developing storms apart before they can strengthen. For hurricane-weary islands, this might sound like good news. But the reality is far more complicated.

El Nino brings severe drought conditions to much of the Caribbean. Jamaica, Barbados, Trinidad and Tobago, and other islands experience significantly reduced rainfall during El Nino events, leading to water shortages, failed crops, and serious threats to food security. Farmers across the region have reported devastating losses as rainfall deficits stretch for months.

Coral reefs — among the Caribbean's most vital ecosystems and a cornerstone of its tourism economy — suffer dramatically during El Nino. Warmer ocean temperatures cause mass coral bleaching events, where stressed corals expel the algae living in their tissues and turn white. Prolonged bleaching kills coral. The Caribbean has lost more than half of its coral reef cover over the past four decades, and El Nino events accelerate that destruction.

The transition period after El Nino ends is also dangerous. As the climate system swings back toward neutral or La Nina conditions — El Nino's cooler counterpart — Atlantic hurricane seasons can intensify dramatically. The Caribbean goes from drought to the threat of catastrophic storms in a matter of months, leaving communities with little time to recover between crises.
The Americas — Floods, Fires, and Famine

Across North and South America, El Nino writes very different stories depending on where you are. The western United States and the Gulf Coast typically see heavier than normal rainfall during El Nino events, which can bring much-needed relief to drought-stricken regions like California — but also devastating floods, mudslides, and infrastructure damage to communities unprepared for the deluge.

The Pacific Northwest and Canada, by contrast, tend to experience warmer and drier conditions during El Nino. This amplifies wildfire risk in forests already stressed by years of drought and rising temperatures. The catastrophic wildfire seasons that have become a defining feature of North American summers in recent years are closely linked to El Nino's drying influence combined with the background warming driven by climate change.

In South America, the impacts are severe and wide-ranging. Peru and Ecuador face extreme flooding and landslides as unusually warm Pacific waters supercharge rainfall along the coast. Brazil's northeastern region, already one of the most drought-prone areas on earth, suffers prolonged dry spells that devastate agriculture and push communities into food insecurity. The Amazon — the planet's largest rainforest and a critical carbon sink — faces increased fire risk as dry conditions spread.

Central America faces some of the most acute food security challenges. The Dry Corridor — a stretch running through Guatemala, Honduras, El Salvador, and Nicaragua — is particularly vulnerable to El Nino-related drought. Smallholder farmers who depend on seasonal rains lose entire harvests. Malnutrition rates rise. Migration pressures increase as communities can no longer sustain themselves on the land.
The United Kingdom — The Ripple Effect Reaches Europe

The United Kingdom sits far from the Pacific Ocean, but El Nino's reach extends even to northwest Europe through complex atmospheric connections. Research has shown that strong El Nino events are associated with wetter and milder winters in the UK and northwestern Europe, as the jet stream — the high-altitude band of fast-moving air that steers weather systems — shifts position in response to changes in tropical weather patterns.

For the UK this means increased flood risk during El Nino years. Rivers overflow. Coastal communities face storm surge threats. Infrastructure built for a climate that no longer exists struggles to cope. The UK has already experienced a series of record-breaking flood events in recent years, and El Nino amplifies the atmospheric conditions that produce them.

Summers following strong El Nino events can bring exceptional heat to Europe. The record-breaking heatwaves that have struck the continent in recent years — killing tens of thousands of people, buckling railway lines, and overwhelming healthcare systems — occur in a climate system where El Nino has contributed to pushing global temperatures to new highs. The summer of 2023, which followed one of the strongest El Nino events in decades, was the hottest in recorded history across much of the Northern Hemisphere.
The Planet — A System Under Stress

Zoom out to the planetary level and the picture becomes even more alarming. El Nino events add a temporary boost of warmth on top of the long-term warming trend driven by human greenhouse gas emissions. During strong El Nino years, global average temperatures can be pushed 0.2 to 0.3 degrees Celsius above the already elevated baseline. In a world that is already 1.2 degrees warmer than pre-industrial levels, this matters enormously.

The years 2023 and 2024 were the hottest ever recorded in human history — driven in part by a powerful El Nino event on top of decades of accumulated warming. Sea surface temperatures reached unprecedented levels. Antarctic sea ice fell to record lows. Glaciers retreated further and faster than any previous measurement had captured. The Greenland ice sheet lost mass at rates that alarmed scientists who had studied it for decades.

Global food systems face mounting pressure. El Nino disrupts agricultural production in multiple major food-producing regions simultaneously — not just in the developing world, but in breadbasket nations whose output feeds billions. When droughts hit the US Midwest, floods devastate South Asian rice paddies, and East African harvests fail all in the same year, the cascading effects on global food prices and food security are severe.

Ocean ecosystems are under extraordinary stress. Marine heatwaves — stretches of ocean where temperatures remain far above normal for weeks or months — have become more frequent and more intense. They bleach coral reefs, disrupt fish migration patterns, cause mass die-offs of marine mammals, and destabilize the food webs that billions of people depend on for their protein.
Climate Change Is Making It Worse

Here is the critical distinction that must be understood: El Nino is a natural phenomenon. It has been occurring for thousands of years. But climate change — driven by the burning of fossil fuels and the resulting buildup of carbon dioxide and other greenhouse gases in the atmosphere — is fundamentally altering how El Nino behaves and what it does to the planet.

A warmer ocean is a more energetic ocean. A warmer atmosphere holds more moisture. When El Nino triggers rainfall, it now triggers heavier rainfall — because the atmosphere can carry and dump more water than it could in a cooler era. When El Nino triggers drought, it now triggers more severe drought — because higher temperatures accelerate evaporation and dry the land faster. The natural cycle has not changed, but the world it operates in has. And that world is now running hotter than at any point in the past 125,000 years.
We are no longer just dealing with El Nino. We are dealing with El Nino in a world our grandparents would not recognize — and our grandchildren may not survive in without urgent action.
What Needs to Happen

The science is settled. The evidence is overwhelming. What is lacking is not knowledge but political will and collective urgency. Reducing greenhouse gas emissions remains the most critical long-term intervention — every fraction of a degree of warming prevented is lives saved, ecosystems preserved, and communities protected. But the speed of current climate change also demands immediate adaptation investment.

Small island developing states — including those across the Caribbean — must be centered in global climate finance discussions. These communities contribute minimally to global emissions but bear disproportionate consequences. The loss and damage funding mechanisms agreed at recent international climate summits must be fully funded and rapidly deployed.

Early warning systems for extreme weather events must be expanded globally, particularly in developing nations where communities have the least capacity to prepare for and recover from climate disasters. Infrastructure must be redesigned for the climate we now have, not the one we had fifty years ago. Agriculture must be reimagined for a more volatile, less predictable growing season.
And the world's media — including platforms like this one — must cover climate change not as an occasional feature story but as the defining crisis of our time. Because it is.
El Nino is nature speaking. Climate change is the language we taught it. The question is whether we will listen before it is too late.

articles.subtitle: Episode 1 of Underreported: Weh Dem Nuh Seh — host Sasha Hemmings exposes the global hunger emergency being deliberately ignored by mainstream media.
articles.author: Sasha Gaye Hemmings | LWS Media Network

articles.excerpt: Episode 1 of Underreported: Weh Dem Nuh Seh. Host Sasha Hemmings breaks down the global hunger crisis — why it is happening, who is responsible, and why the world is staying silent.

body.text: Welcome to Underreported: Weh Dem Nuh Seh — where we spotlight the global stories that are not trending but should be. In this episode, host Sasha Hemmings breaks down the global hunger emergency that mainstream media continues to ignore.

body.caption: UNDERREPORTED: The Hidden Hunger Crisis — Why the World Is Silent. Hosted by Sasha Hemmings.

articles.title: UNDERREPORTED: The Global Hunger Crisis No One Is Talking About

articles.author: Sasha Gaye Hemmings | LWS Media Network

articles.excerpt: Episode 2 of Underreported: Weh Dem Nuh Seh. From Sudan to Gaza, the Sahel to Haiti, humanitarian food programs are collapsing — and the world stays silent.

body.text: In this episode of Underreported: Weh Dem Nuh Seh, host Sasha Hemmings exposes the silent crisis affecting over 783 million people worldwide. From Sudan to Gaza, the Sahel to Haiti, humanitarian food programs are collapsing due to lack of funding, political indifference, and conflict-driven blockades. Entire regions are being starved into submission — and yet the world stays silent.

body.caption: UNDERREPORTED: The Global Hunger Crisis No One Is Talking About — Hosted by Sasha Hemmings.

body.text: What You Will Learn in This Episode

How politics and profit decide who gets to eat. Why famine is a man-made disaster, not a natural one. The real reasons hunger is not making headlines. And what you can do to raise awareness and make a difference.
Hunger is not just a humanitarian issue — it is a weapon of war. This is the story the world needs to hear.
Underreported: Weh Dem Nuh Seh is a series by LWS Media Network where we bring light to what others ignore. Stay alert, stay aware, and stay loud for those who cannot be heard.

articles.title: Weh Yuh Seh Views — Morning Briefing | September 3, 2025
articles.subtitle: Global news you can trust in just a few minutes — from drone strikes over Ukraine to AI safety updates, climate alerts, and the week's most important diplomatic moves.
articles.author: Sasha Gaye Hemmings | LWS Media Network
articles.coverImageCaption: Weh Yuh Seh Views — Morning Briefing, September 3, 2025. Photo: LWS Media Network
articles.excerpt: Good morning. Today's briefing covers Russia's massive overnight drone and missile barrage on Ukraine, NATO's response, markets on edge over bond yields, new AI parental controls, and climate alerts across Europe and the US.

body.text: Good morning, and welcome to Weh Yuh Seh Views. I am your host, Sasha — bringing you global news you can trust in just a few minutes. Here are today's headlines for September 3, 2025.
1. Top Global Headlines

Russia unleashed a massive drone and missile barrage overnight — over 500 drones and dozens of missiles struck Ukrainian cities, injuring railway workers and disrupting critical infrastructure. NATO jets were deployed to patrol Polish airspace in response to the escalation.

NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte has indicated he anticipates clarity on security guarantees for Ukraine at an upcoming summit co-hosted by French President Macron and UK Prime Minister Starmer. A peacekeeping force is among the options being evaluated to deter future Russian aggression.
2. Breaking News

US President is set to welcome Poland's President Nawrocki at the White House today. Talks are expected to focus on strengthening defense cooperation, energy resilience, and NATO ties — all against the backdrop of the ongoing conflict in Ukraine.
3. Politics and Diplomacy

Ukrainian President Zelenskiy is heading to Denmark and France to lobby allies for stronger pressure on Russia following the latest wave of airstrikes. European powers are actively evaluating postwar security frameworks as the conflict shows no signs of resolution.
4. Business and Finance

Markets remain cautious as global bond yields — especially in the United Kingdom — hit their highest levels in decades. US markets are awaiting reaction to Nvidia's earnings report and the latest moves on tariffs and technology export restrictions.
5. Technology and AI

New parental control features have been released for ChatGPT, aiming to enhance safety and content moderation for young users. The update comes amid growing pressure on AI companies to do more to protect children online.
6. Climate and Weather Alerts

Europe continues to suffer from devastating wildfire conditions, with the season potentially still producing new hotspots across the continent. Climate scientists warn that this pattern is becoming the new normal as temperatures continue to rise.

US Regional Forecast for September 3, 2025 — Florida: Still hot and humid, expect 93 degrees Fahrenheit (34 Celsius) with possible afternoon storms. Georgia: Warm at 91 degrees Fahrenheit (33 Celsius) with showers likely. New York: Mild evening at 72 degrees Fahrenheit (22 Celsius) with heavy rain expected.
7. Science and Health

The death toll from the Afghanistan earthquake has been estimated at over 1,400, with rescue operations underway amid widespread devastation. Aid organisations are calling for urgent international support as affected communities struggle to access basic services.
8. Special Reports

Russia and Belarus are set to conduct joint military exercises called Zapad 2025 from September 12 to 16. The drills will feature scenarios involving nuclear deployment and are raising serious concerns among NATO and European Union observers about the signal being sent at this critical moment in the conflict.
That is your Morning Briefing for September 3, 2025. This is Weh Yuh Seh Views — delivering the stories shaping our world, every morning and evening. See you at 5 PM for the Evening Digest.
body.cite: Sasha Gaye Hemmings, LWS Media Network

articles.title: Meet Samara: The 9-Year-Old Behind a Global Learning Platform
articles.subtitle: From YouTube educator to the face of an AI-powered edtech platform reaching children worldwide — Samara's story is redefining what it means to grow up building something that matters.
articles.author: LWS Media
articles.coverImageCaption: Samara, photographed for Learning With Samara — May 2026. Photo: LWS Media Network
articles.excerpt: At nine years old, most children are thinking about their favourite cartoons. Samara is thinking about curriculum design and how to make learning feel less like a chore for children around the world.

body.text: At nine years old, most children are thinking about their favourite cartoons, their next playdate, or what is for dinner. Samara is thinking about curriculum design, user experience, and how to make learning feel less like a chore for children around the world.

She is the inspiration behind Learning With Samara — an AI-powered educational platform built by her mother, solo founder Sasha Gaye Hemmings, and the flagship product of Geor De Mer LLC. But Samara is far more than a namesake. She is the platform's most enthusiastic tester, its most honest critic, and increasingly, its most compelling ambassador.
I wanted kids to actually enjoy learning. Not just sit there and be bored.
Growing Up on Camera

Long before the platform launched, Samara was already building an audience on YouTube through Samara TV — a channel dedicated to animated learning videos designed to make education engaging for very young children. The channel became both a proof of concept and a creative outlet, one where Samara's personality could shine through every video.

body.caption: Watch: Samara TV — the YouTube channel that started it all.

body.text: The Platform

Learning With Samara covers children from ages 0 to 17, offering AI-powered tutoring, gamified learning, PEP Jamaica curriculum prep, ESL for non-English speakers, and a growing library of interactive games and activities. All of it free to start, with a Pro plan that unlocks the full experience.
What Comes Next

With the platform now available globally and a Google Play Store launch on the horizon, Samara's story is only just beginning. For her mother and founder Sasha, building in public with her daughter by her side has always been about more than technology — it is about showing the next generation that they do not have to wait to start building.

body.caption: Samara and her solar system science project.
Watch: Samara in action — a glimpse of the energy behind the platform.
Mother and daughter — the team behind it all.

articles.title: Building From the Caribbean — How Island Entrepreneurs Are Going Global
articles.subtitle: From Kingston to Bridgetown, a new generation of Caribbean founders is building technology, lifestyle, and media brands that compete on the world stage — without waiting for the world's permission.
articles.author: LWS Media Network
articles.coverImageCaption: Caribbean entrepreneurs are rewriting the rules of global business. Photo: LWS Media Network
articles.excerpt: From Kingston to Bridgetown, a new generation of Caribbean founders is building technology, lifestyle, and media brands that compete on the world stage — without waiting for the world's permission.

body.text: There is a revolution happening in the Caribbean — and it is not making the front pages of the world's major business publications. It is happening in apartments in Kingston, home offices in Port of Spain, co-working spaces in Bridgetown, and kitchen tables in Nassau. It is being built by a generation of Caribbean entrepreneurs who have decided, collectively and quietly, that they do not need the world's permission to go global.

For too long, the narrative around Caribbean economies has been dominated by two things: tourism and crisis. The region's entrepreneurs have been largely invisible in the global conversation about innovation, technology, and the future of business. But the data — and the businesses themselves — tell a very different story.
A Movement Building in Plain Sight

Across the Caribbean, founders are building products and platforms with users in dozens of countries. They are launching e-commerce brands shipping to North America, Europe, and beyond. They are creating digital media companies, fintech solutions, edtech platforms, and lifestyle brands that speak to a global diaspora hungry for content and products that reflect their identity.

What makes this movement remarkable is not just what is being built — it is how it is being built. Many of these founders are operating as solo entrepreneurs or very small teams, leveraging free and low-cost digital tools to compete with companies that have significantly more resources. They are scrappy, resourceful, and deeply connected to the communities they serve.
We were never behind. We were just building in a room the world had not looked into yet.
body.cite: Caribbean Entrepreneur, LWS Media Network Feature

body.text: The Diaspora as a Global Market

One of the most powerful — and underestimated — advantages Caribbean entrepreneurs have is their connection to the diaspora. There are an estimated six million people of Caribbean descent living in the United States alone, with significant communities in the United Kingdom, Canada, and across Europe. This is not a niche market. It is a global one.

Caribbean founders who build with their community in mind do not just serve the islands — they serve a worldwide network of people who share a cultural identity, a set of values, and a hunger for products, media, and services that speak directly to them. This is a competitive advantage that no Silicon Valley startup can easily replicate.

The businesses thriving on this understanding span every sector. Food and beverage brands bringing authentic Caribbean flavours to international shelves. Beauty and wellness companies rooted in natural island ingredients. Digital platforms serving Caribbean students, professionals, and families no matter where in the world they have landed.
Technology Is the Great Equaliser

A decade ago, building a global business from a small island meant navigating enormous barriers — limited access to capital, distance from major markets, and the perception that serious business only happened in major cities. Technology has not eliminated these barriers entirely, but it has reduced them dramatically.

Today, a founder in Jamaica can build a software product used by customers in fifty countries. A designer in Trinidad can serve clients in London, New York, and Dubai without ever boarding a plane. An educator in Barbados can reach students across the globe through a digital platform launched from a laptop. The geography that once limited Caribbean entrepreneurs has become increasingly irrelevant.

Artificial intelligence has accelerated this shift further. Founders who once needed large teams to compete can now use AI tools to handle everything from customer service to content creation, allowing them to focus on what they do best — building something that matters.
The Challenges That Remain

This is not a story without obstacles. Caribbean entrepreneurs still face real and significant challenges. Access to venture capital remains limited compared to founders in North America or Europe. Banking infrastructure in many island nations can make it difficult to accept international payments or access business credit. And the persistent underrepresentation of Caribbean voices in global business media means that many of these founders are building without the visibility, networks, or validation that their counterparts elsewhere take for granted.

There is also the challenge of brain drain — the ongoing migration of talented young people from the Caribbean to larger economies in search of opportunity. Every Caribbean founder who builds something extraordinary at home is, in a small but meaningful way, pushing back against that tide.
What the World Is Missing

The global business conversation is poorer for its failure to pay attention to what is being built in the Caribbean. The region's entrepreneurs bring a perspective shaped by resilience, creativity, and community that the rest of the world would benefit from understanding. They have been building in difficult conditions for generations — and they are very good at it.

The next time a major publication runs a feature on the world's most exciting startup ecosystems, the Caribbean deserves to be in that conversation. Not as an afterthought. Not as a feel-good story about a small island punching above its weight. But as a legitimate, vibrant, and growing part of the global innovation economy.
The Caribbean has always produced people who build. The world is just starting to pay attention.
The Story Is Just Beginning

LWS Media Network will be telling these stories. The founders building quietly from their islands. The diaspora entrepreneurs turning cultural identity into global brands. The innovators solving problems the rest of the world has not even noticed yet. This is a movement worth watching — and worth amplifying.
If you are a Caribbean entrepreneur building something extraordinary, we want to hear your story. Because the world needs to hear it too.

articles.title: The Hidden Hunger Crisis — Why the World Is Quiet About Starvation
articles.subtitle: Over 783 million people are going to sleep hungry tonight. This is not a story about poverty — it is a story about silence, indifference, and the deliberate turning away of the world's most powerful nations.
articles.author: Sasha Gaye Hemmings | LWS Media Network
articles.coverImageCaption: Underreported: Weh Dem Nuh Seh — stories the world chooses not to tell. Photo: LWS Media Network
articles.excerpt: Over 783 million people are going to sleep hungry tonight. In Sudan, Gaza, and across the Sahel, humanitarian food programs are being cut — not because the need is not there, but because the world simply is not paying attention.

body.text: Welcome to Underreported: Weh Dem Nuh Seh — where we spotlight the global stories that are not trending but should be. Today, we are talking about a quiet crisis — one the world is deliberately ignoring: global hunger.
A Crisis in the Shadows
Right now, over 783 million people are going to sleep hungry. But this is not just about poverty — it is about silence.

In Sudan, Gaza, and across the Sahel, humanitarian food programs are being cut due to lack of funding. Not because the need is not there — but because the world simply is not paying attention. In Sudan alone, 23 million people are food insecure, and children are dying from malnutrition at double the rate compared to just last year.
Aid groups like the World Food Programme say they are being forced to choose who eats and who starves — not because they want to, but because donor governments have turned their backs.
Hunger does not just kill the body. It breaks the spirit. And silence, in the face of suffering, makes us complicit.
body.cite: Sasha Gaye Hemmings, LWS Media Network

body.text: Why Aren't We Talking About This?

Why is this not making headlines? Because most of the people affected are in Africa, the Middle East, or politically unstable regions. Because there is no viral hashtag for starving children in Burkina Faso or Yemen. And because funding flows based on politics and profit — not compassion.
Even in places like Haiti, gang violence has blocked entire neighborhoods from receiving aid. That is not just hunger — that is organized starvation. And the world's media cycle has moved on.
What You Need to Know

Let us be clear: famine is not caused by food shortages. It is caused by conflict, climate change, and most of all — indifference. This is a man-made disaster, driven by decisions made in powerful rooms and the lack of accountability in global institutions.

If this were happening in Europe or North America, we would see 24/7 coverage. Rolling news. Emergency summits. But for now, hunger has become a silent weapon of war — and the silence of the international community is deafening.
Where Do We Go From Here?

This is not a problem without solutions. But solutions require attention, and attention requires voices. Stay informed and share stories like this one. Support independent journalism and credible humanitarian organisations. Hold governments accountable for how they fund — or defund — crisis zones.
The world needs louder voices, not just louder noise. The people going hungry tonight do not need our pity — they need our action, our attention, and our refusal to look away.
This is Underreported: Weh Dem Nuh Seh — where we bring light to what others ignore. Stay alert, stay aware, and stay loud for those who cannot be heard.
body.cite: Sasha Gaye Hemmings, LWS Media Network

articles.title: The Slow Drowning of Freshwater — How Saltwater Intrusion Threatens Our Future
articles.subtitle: From Florida to Bangladesh, seawater is quietly seeping into our underground freshwater supplies. There is no dramatic footage, no viral moment — just a slow, invisible poisoning of the most essential resource on earth.
articles.author: Sasha Gaye Hemmings | LWS Media Network
articles.coverImageCaption: Underreported: Weh Dem Nuh Seh — the slow crisis contaminating our drinking water. Photo: LWS Media Network
articles.excerpt: From Florida to Bangladesh, seawater is quietly seeping into underground freshwater supplies. Once salt enters these aquifers it is almost impossible to remove — and millions of people are already drinking the consequences.

body.text: Welcome to Underreported: Weh Dem Nuh Seh — where we spotlight the global stories that are not trending but should be. Today we are talking about a creeping disaster that is quietly contaminating our drinking water and putting millions at risk: saltwater intrusion.
A Crisis in the Shadows
Across coastal regions from Florida to Bangladesh, seawater is slowly seeping into underground freshwater supplies. This is not a sudden flood — it is a quiet, invisible invasion.

In Miami, the salt line is moving inland at around 330 feet each year. In parts of Bangladesh and island nations across the Pacific, wells that once provided fresh, safe water now taste like the ocean. And once salt enters these aquifers, it is almost impossible to remove.
For communities that rely on these water sources, this means drinking water that raises blood pressure, damages kidneys, and corrodes infrastructure — from household plumbing to entire city systems.
Once the freshwater source is contaminated, communities must rely on costly desalination or imported water — both of which can push poor regions deeper into crisis.
body.cite: Sasha Gaye Hemmings, LWS Media Network

body.text: Why Aren't We Talking About This?
This is not making headlines because it is slow, technical, and invisible. There is no dramatic footage, no single moment of disaster — just a gradual poisoning of life's most essential resource.

It also disproportionately affects poorer coastal communities — in places like Southeast Asia, the Caribbean, and rural US coastal towns — where political influence is low and media coverage is rare. Solving it requires massive investment in infrastructure and climate adaptation — something many governments would rather ignore until it is too late.
What You Need to Know

Saltwater intrusion is not caused by nature alone. It is fuelled by rising sea levels from climate change, over-pumping of groundwater which draws seawater inland, and weak regulation of coastal development.

The hidden danger is that once the freshwater source is gone, the economic consequences are devastating. Communities must turn to costly desalination or imported water — both of which are out of reach for the world's poorest regions. This is not just an environmental issue. It is a public health emergency and an economic time bomb.
Where Do We Go From Here?

This slow-moving crisis is one we can stop — but only if we see it for what it is: a direct threat to human survival. Governments must invest in monitoring and protecting freshwater aquifers before it is too late. Climate adaptation projects in vulnerable coastal regions need urgent funding and international support.
The world is slowly losing one of its most precious resources — and most people have no idea it is happening. That is exactly why stories like this one need to be told.
This is Underreported: Weh Dem Nuh Seh — where we bring light to what others ignore. Stay alert, stay aware, and stay loud for those who cannot be heard.
body.cite: Sasha Gaye Hemmings, LWS Media Network
